entries.when_to_use: When the goal is to melt a cold stick of butter into liquid in a kitchen with a saucepan and stove available.
entries.strategy: Put the butter in the saucepan, place the saucepan on a stove burner, turn the burner to low heat, and let it warm briefly until the butter fully melts. Use low heat to melt gently and avoid burning.

entries.when_to_use: When the goal is to bring a pot of cold water to a rolling boil on a stovetop.
entries.strategy: Place the pot securely on an appropriate burner, turn that burner to high heat, then wait while monitoring. Small bubbles mean it is heating but not yet at a rolling boil; continue heating until large bubbles rapidly break across the surface.

entries.when_to_use: When frying a raw egg sunny-side-up in a non-stick pan with butter on a stove.
entries.strategy: Place the pan on the burner, heat it gently on medium-low, melt a small pat of butter to coat the surface without browning, then crack in the egg carefully to keep the yolk intact. Cook undisturbed on medium-low until the whites are fully set but the yolk remains runny, then turn off the burner promptly.

entries.when_to_use: When the goal is to freeze liquid water into solid ice and a freezer plus an empty ice tray or similar freezer-safe mold is available.
entries.strategy: Pour the water into the ice tray without spilling, open the freezer, place the filled tray level on a shelf, close the freezer to maintain temperature, and wait several hours until the water freezes solid.

entries.when_to_use: When combining flour, salt, and water into a uniform dough in a bowl.
entries.strategy: Add flour and salt first, stir the dry ingredients to distribute the salt evenly, then add water gradually while stirring. If dry patches remain, add small amounts of water directly to them and continue stirring and pressing until the mixture comes together into one uniform dough.

entries.when_to_use: When melting chocolate for dipping in a kitchen with a stove, saucepan, water, and a heat-safe bowl available.
entries.strategy: Use a gentle double boiler: put a shallow amount of water in the saucepan, bring it to a gentle simmer, break the chocolate into small pieces in the heat-safe bowl, set the bowl securely over the saucepan without touching the water, and stir steadily until the chocolate is smooth and glossy. Keep the heat gentle to avoid scorching or overheating.

entries.when_to_use: When a pot of cold soup or similar liquid food needs to be brought to a vigorous boil on a stove and the burner is initially off.
entries.strategy: Place the pot centered on a stove burner, turn the burner to high, and keep heating on high until large bubbles rapidly break across the surface. If only steam or small edge bubbles appear, continue heating until a rolling/vigorous boil is reached.

entries.when_to_use: When cooking 1 cup of dry rice in a pot on a stove with water and a lid, and the goal is tender, fluffy rice.
entries.strategy: Put the rice in the pot, add about 2 cups of water, bring it to a boil on high heat, then cover the pot and reduce heat to low. Simmer undisturbed for about 18 minutes until water is absorbed, turn off the heat, let the covered pot rest for about 5 minutes, then remove the lid and fluff the rice.

entries.when_to_use: When a sealed bottle or can of soda is warm and the goal is to make it cold to the touch quickly, with access to a freezer.
entries.strategy: Place the soda in the freezer briefly, wait about 20 minutes, then remove it promptly before it can freeze, expand, or rupture. Verify success by touching the outside of the container to confirm it feels cold.

entries.when_to_use: When making an omelette base from raw eggs and milk in a mixing bowl, with a whisk available.
entries.strategy: Crack the eggs into the bowl first, ensuring no shell pieces are visible; pour in the milk; whisk briskly. If streaks of yolk, white, or milk remain, continue whisking until the mixture is smooth, pale yellow, and uniform.

entries.when_to_use: When melting a block of cheddar cheese into a sauce in a kitchen with a saucepan, stove, milk, and whisk.
entries.strategy: Add a small amount of milk to the saucepan, warm it gently on low heat, then add the cheddar. Whisk continuously over low heat until the cheese breaks down and the mixture becomes smooth; continue gentle whisking if small lumps remain.

entries.when_to_use: When heating milk to a bare simmer on a stove and avoiding scalding is important.
entries.strategy: Use low heat, stir slowly and frequently to prevent hot spots and sticking, and monitor with a thermometer held in the milk without touching the pot bottom. As the milk approaches 180°F and shows steady steam with tiny edge bubbles, turn off the heat; a final temperature around 180–185°F indicates a bare simmer without boiling or scalding.

entries.when_to_use: When searing a raw steak to medium-rare with a crusted exterior using a heavy pan such as cast iron and high-smoke-point oil.
entries.strategy: Pat the steak dry first, preheat the cast-iron pan over high heat until very hot, add only a thin layer of high-smoke-point oil until it shimmers, then place the steak in and leave it undisturbed for about 3 minutes per side. Flip once with tongs, remove when both sides are browned and the steak feels springy with some resistance, then rest it for about 5 minutes so juices redistribute and carryover heat finishes the center.

entries.when_to_use: When warm chocolate ganache needs to be turned into a firm, scoopable truffle filling and a refrigerator plus shallow dish are available.
entries.strategy: Pour the warm ganache into a shallow dish so it forms a thin, even layer, then chill it level in the refrigerator. Check after it thickens; if it is not yet firm, continue refrigerating until it is cool, matte, and holds its shape when lightly touched. Avoid using the freezer for this unless rapid hardening is specifically desired, since gentle refrigeration helps preserve texture.
